Q: Practiced heavily in Quant, estimating more than 2,000 Quant questions because it was the preferred section.
Used GMAT Club filters for 705-805 and 805+ questions and did streaks of hard questions.
Compared real Quant as slightly easier than Experts' Global and roughly near later official mocks.
Accepted a 675 rather than retaking only for the 700 mark, choosing to spend time improving the broader profile.
V: Worked on reading questions once carefully enough to avoid rereading, which saved time.
Finished the official Verbal section with about five minutes to spare.
DI: Targeted weak DI areas, especially non-math TPA, between mocks.
Used regular GMAT Club quizzes to keep timing sharp across all sections.
Practiced DI quizzes by trying to complete the same set in about 40 minutes.
Found two text-heavy MSRs on test day, which strained timing despite practice.
overall: Prepared for six months while working, usually two hours on weekdays and five effective hours on weekends.
Self-studied with GMAT Club, Experts' Global mocks, official mocks 1, 2, 5, and 6, and the Official Guide.
Started with weekly mocks, then reduced frequency as official mocks became scarce.
Used the time between mocks to target weak areas rather than only taking more tests.
Entered the exam after mock scores of 705, 695, 725, 715, and 685 but accepted the final 675 as sufficient for applications.

Q: Used GMAT Club as the primary practice source.
Did not believe a strong first section necessarily made later sections harder; after strong Verbal, Quant felt easier than in previous attempts.
V: Broke a plateau by reducing overthinking on early Verbal questions.
In a prior 675 attempt, spent so long early that only 16 or 17 questions were reached with two minutes left.
In the successful attempt, reached the 22nd Verbal question by removing early overthinking.
For RC, focused on big picture rather than trying to remember every small detail.
DI: Used GMAT Club as the DI source.
overall: Reached 715 on the seventh attempt after plateauing at 675 for three attempts.
Mock scores had regularly been 705-735, so the issue was not knowledge but test discipline.
Changed discipline rather than study materials.
Viewed the real exam as tougher than mocks, possibly because of test-room pressure.
Reported 85-90% GMAT Club practice accuracy.

Q: Practiced all OG questions and used e-GMAT sectional mocks to adapt to timing.
Practiced Quant much more than Verbal or DI.
Used all three answer edits early in the official Quant section to correct initial mistakes and reach Q90.
V: Used GMAT Ninja as the most helpful Verbal resource.
Kept Verbal confidence because mock Verbal scores consistently ranged from 83 to 86.
Used section order Quant, Verbal, break, DI.
DI: Used e-GMAT sectional mocks periodically to adapt to timing.
Kept DI expectations stable because mock DI performance was similar to final exam performance.
Used section order Quant, Verbal, break, DI.
overall: Improved from a 475 e-GMAT diagnostic to 715 after two and a half months of serious full-time preparation.
Used e-GMAT for concepts, then GMAT Ninja videos and GMAT Club streams for additional support.
Saved official mocks until the end because only a limited number were available.
Tried Sigma-X mocks but found them less representative, then bought all four paid official mocks and stuck with official mocks.
Took seven mocks total, with official mock scores mostly 665-685 before the final 715.
Prepared full time with at least three hours per day.

Q: Used about 150 hours of TTP, finding it especially helpful for Quant.
After TTP chapter tests, used flashcards and practice questions from various PDFs.
Reduced silly mistakes by categorizing each Quant question before solving, such as rate changes, unit conversions, or algebra.
Applied the correct mental framework once the question type was identified.
Used mental math improvement and repeated practice to reduce missed steps.
DI: Found the official DI section very hard compared with expectations.
overall: Used TTP, flashcards, PDF practice, and the two free official mocks before the exam.
Took the exam a few days after the two free mocks.
Scored lower than mock percentiles and debated a retake, but recognized diminishing returns above 715.
Weighed further GMAT optimization against the opportunity cost of improving the rest of the application.

Q: Used TTP to rebuild Quant after a decade away from math-heavy work.
Followed TTP's daily study plan closely and used review tests to keep concepts active.
After TTP, completed OG guides while adding GMAT Club Quant sectional tests.
Maintained a meticulous Quant error log and worked to reduce careless errors.
Used higher-difficulty GMAT Club forum tests even though they initially felt intimidating.
In the final 10 days, reviewed Quant error logs, TTP notes, formulae, and active review sheets instead of broad new practice.
Took custom GMAT Club tests only on the weakest Quant topics near the end.
V: Started with strong natural Verbal but lacked a repeatable CR strategy.
Used PowerScore CR Bible and e-GMAT free-trial CR lessons to add structure.
Combined TTP's non-prethinking style with e-GMAT's negation and pre-thinking techniques where useful.
Used a structured CR process rather than relying only on general English skill.
Changed section order to Verbal, Quant, DI after testing strongest-to-weakest sequencing.
DI: Identified DI volatility as a major issue after initial official practice tests.
Used OG and sectional reviews for DI after completing TTP.
Applied GMAT Club's time-management strategy to stabilize pacing.
Changed section order to Verbal, Quant, DI, which made sectional scores more predictable and improved timing.
overall: Prepared for about six months from a 575 diagnostic to a 695 first official attempt while working in clinical dentistry.
Used GMAT Ninja videos initially to understand the exam, then TTP, GMAT Club, OG guides, PowerScore, and e-GMAT free-trial materials.
Studied no more than about four to five hours per day and avoided putting life fully on pause.
After a sudden mock drop from 705 to 645, recognized burnout and took a two-week break from GMAT.
Returned with meditation, yoga, and a more sustainable routine rather than a completely new study plan.
For the final 10 days, stopped broad practice, reviewed logs and notes, narrowed weak topics, and kept normal life intact.
Simulated test-day tools during mocks and treated the official exam like another practice test to reduce anxiety.
